journal-title-group.journal-title: Current Therapeutic Research, Clinical and Experimental

title-group.article-title: Efficacy of Orlistat on Cardiometabolic Indices in Patients With Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD): A GRADE-Assessed Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of RCTs

contrib-group.aff: aFaculty of Medicine, Bogomolets National Medical University, Kyiv, Ukraine
bSchool of Medicine, Tehran University of Medical Sciences (TUMS), Tehran, Iran
cFaculty of Medicine, Islamic Azad University, Tehran Medical Sciences Branch (IAUTMU), Tehran, Iran

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background and Aim
sec.p: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) is closely linked to cardiometabolic abnormalities. Orlistat has demonstrated potential benefits on metabolic parameters, yet its efficacy in MASLD remains unclear. This meta-analysis aimed to assess the effects of Orlistat on cardiometabolic indices in patients with MASLD.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: To identify relevant randomized controlled trials (RCTs), a comprehensive search was conducted across major databases from inception to January 2026. Outcomes assessed included aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT), triglycerides (TG), total cholesterol (TC), low- and high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C, HDL-C), fasting blood glucose (FBG), fasting insulin (FI), homeostatic model assessment for insulin resistance (HOMA-IR), body mass index (BMI), systolic and diastolic blood pressure (BP). Pooled effect sizes were calculated using a random-effects model using weighted mean differences (WMD) with 95% confidence intervals (CI).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Six RCTs were included in this meta-analysis. The pooled analysis showed that Orlistat significantly improved liver enzymes, with reductions in AST (WMD: −4.01 U/L, 95% CI: −6.05 to −1.97, P = 0.005), ALT (WMD: −8.70 U/L, 95% CI: −16.51 to −0.90, P = 0.036), and GGT (WMD: −7.74 U/L, 95% CI: −14.88 to −0.59, P = 0.04). It also improved lipid profile by lowering LDL-C (WMD: −5.61 mg/dL, 95% CI: −7.36 to −3.86, P = 0.002) and increasing HDL-C in long-term interventions (≥24 months, WMD: 1.65 mg/dL, 95% CI: 0.95–2.36, P = 0.02). Glycemic markers improved, with significant reductions in FBS (WMD: −4.05 mg/dL, 95% CI: −6.32 to −1.78, P = 0.005), FI (WMD: −2.50 μU/mL, 95% CI: −3.46 to −1.55, P = 0.003), and HOMA-IR (WMD: −0.70, 95% CI: −0.98 to −0.41, P = 0.004). Orlistat did not significantly affect BP. BMI decreased significantly overall (WMD: −0.99 kg/m², 95% CI: −1.18 to −0.81, P < 0.001), with stronger effects in long-term interventions.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Orlistat may improve cardiometabolic and liver function in MASLD patients, significantly reducing liver enzymes, enhancing lipid profiles, improving glycemic control, and lowering BMI. These results indicate its potential as an effective therapeutic option for metabolic management. However, larger, high-quality RCTs with longer follow-up are necessary to confirm its long-term efficacy and safety.

def.p: homeostatic model assessment for insulin resistance

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), formerly known as Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), is the most prevalent chronic liver condition, characterized by excessive fat accumulation in the liver, with a prevalence approaching 25% in the general population.1,2 MASLD is closely associated with metabolic syndrome, obesity, insulin resistance, and dyslipidemia, making it a significant public health concern due to its potential to progress to nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH), cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma.3, 4, 5 Current management primarily relies on lifestyle interventions such as weight reduction and increased physical activity;6, 7, 8 however, growing interest has focused on adjunct pharmacological and nutritional approaches to enhance therapeutic efficacy. In this context, several agents have been identified as potential metabolic regulators in both in vivo and in vitro models.9, 10, 11 Comprehensive reviews on functional foods further emphasize the importance of dietary strategies in MASLD prevention and management, with beneficial implications for metabolic and hepatic outcomes.12,13

Orlistat, a gastrointestinal lipase inhibitor, is widely prescribed for obesity management due to its ability to prevent the absorption of dietary fats, thereby aiding in weight reduction.14,15 When lifestyle changes are insufficient, pharmacologic obesity treatment is a key option for obesity-related MASLD alongside endoscopic techniques and metabolic/bariatric surgery within a precision-medicine framework. Orlistat is one of the available pharmacotherapies.16 Herein, in MASLD, treatment endpoints should extend beyond liver histology to reflect its systemic metabolic nature.17 By reducing fat absorption, orlistat may positively influence lipid metabolism and liver function, offering a promising avenue for MASLD management.18,19

Recent studies suggest that Orlistat may have beneficial effects on liver fat content and overall liver function in MASLD patients, independent of its weight loss effects.20,21 Although previous studies have attempted to evaluate the effect of weight-loss agents in NAFLD/MASLD populations.22 Few have focused specifically on Orlistat, and even fewer have applied rigorous methodological frameworks, such as GRADE, to assess the certainty of the evidence. Furthermore, earlier reviews often lacked stratification by key metabolic indices or did not fully account for variations in study quality, duration, or patient characteristics. This has created a significant gap in understanding the consistency, reliability, and generalizability of Orlistat’s metabolic benefits in MASLD populations.

This systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to evaluate the efficacy of Orlistat in improving liver-related outcomes in MASLD patients, drawing on data from randomized controlled trials (RCTs). By synthesizing available evidence, this study seeks to provide a clearer understanding of Orlistat's potential role in the management of MASLD.

sec.title: Study protocol and registration
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis were conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines.25 The study protocol was prospectively registered in the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO) under registration number CRD42024561140. The methodological design was structured using the PICOS (Population, Intervention, Comparison, Outcomes, and Study design) framework,26 which informed the formulation of eligibility criteria, data extraction procedures, and outcome evaluation throughout the review process. The PICOS framework was defined as follows:

list-item.p: O (Outcomes): Changes in cardiometabolic Indices, including aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT), triglycerides (TG), total cholesterol (TC), low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C), high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C), fasting blood glucose (FBG), fasting insulin (FI), homeostatic model assessment for insulin resistance (HOMA-IR), body mass index (BMI), systolic and diastolic blood pressure (BP).
S (Study Design): Randomized, parallel controlled group trials.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: A systematic and comprehensive literature search was performed across the electronic databases PubMed/MEDLINE, Web of Science, and Scopus to identify relevant studies evaluating the effects of orlistat in MASLD. The search was conducted on January 1st, 2026, using a combination of Medical Subject Headings (MeSH) terms and free-text keywords related to orlistat and steatotic liver disease. The search terms included: (“orlistat” OR “xenical” OR “orlipastatum” OR “tetrahydrolipstatin” OR “venustat”) AND (“fatty liver” OR “liver steatosis” OR “hepatic steatosis” OR “hepatosteatosis” OR “steatohepatitis” OR “steatotic liver” OR “liver disease” OR “metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease” OR “MASLD” OR “nonalcoholic fatty liver disease” OR “NAFLD”).

No restrictions were applied regarding language, publication date, or publication status to maximize the sensitivity of the search. To further enhance comprehensiveness, we also searched grey literature sources, including conference proceedings, clinical trial registries, and theses. However, considering the variable reporting standards and lack of peer review in grey literature, these sources were used primarily to identify potential studies, which were then critically appraised for methodological quality before inclusion. Reference lists of all included studies and relevant review articles were also manually screened to capture additional eligible publications. The complete and database-specific search strategies are presented in the Supplementary Material.

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: Studies were considered eligible for inclusion if they met the following criteria:1 RCTs (parallel-group design);2 conducted in adult participants diagnosed with MASLD or NASH;3 evaluated the effects of orlistat administration as the primary intervention;4 included a placebo or controlled comparison group; and 5 reported at least one predefined cardiometabolic outcome of interest, including liver enzymes (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT), lipid profile parameters (TG, TC, LDL-C, HDL-C), glycemic indices (FBG, FI, HOMA-IR), BMI, or BP outcomes. No restrictions were applied regarding language, publication year, or publication status.

Studies were excluded if they were nonrandomized designs (e.g., observational studies, quasi-experimental trials, case reports, reviews, or animal studies), involved pediatric or adolescent populations, or lacked an appropriate control or placebo group. Trials were also excluded if they failed to report relevant metabolic or cardiometabolic outcomes, provided insufficient data for quantitative synthesis, or investigated orlistat in combination with other pharmacological interventions such that the independent effect of orlistat could not be clearly isolated.

sec.title: Screening and study selection
sec.p: All identified records were imported into EndNote, and duplicate entries were removed. Two independent reviewers (M.K. and M.K.) screened the titles and abstracts of all retrieved studies according to the predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Inter-rater agreement at the title and abstract screening stage was assessed using Cohen’s kappa, which demonstrated a high level of agreement between reviewers. Full texts of potentially eligible studies were subsequently retrieved and independently assessed for eligibility by the same reviewers. Any discrepancies during the screening or selection process were resolved through discussion and, when necessary, consultation with a third reviewer (S.M.A.P.). The study selection process is summarized in the PRISMA flow diagram.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Data extraction was independently conducted by two researchers (M.K. and S.P.) using a standardized data extraction form developed in Microsoft Excel. Extracted information included study characteristics (first author, publication year, and country), participant characteristics (sample size, mean age, sex distribution, and diagnostic criteria for MASLD), intervention details (orlistat dosage, frequency, and duration), characteristics of the control or placebo group, and reported outcomes. Outcomes of interest comprised liver enzymes (AST, ALT, ALP, GGT), lipid profile parameters (TG, TC, LDL-C, HDL-C), glycemic indices (FBG, FI, and HOMA-IR), BMI, and systolic and diastolic BP. Any discrepancies between the reviewers were resolved through discussion.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: The methodological quality and risk of bias of the included studies were assessed using the Cochrane Risk of Bias 2 (ROB 2) tool. The ROB 2 tool evaluates several domains of bias, including the randomization process, deviations from intended interventions, missing outcome data, outcome measurement, and selection of the reported result.27 Two independent reviewers (M.K. & S.P.) conducted the quality assessment, and disagreements were resolved through consensus or consultation.

sec.title: Certainty assessment
sec.p: The overall certainty of evidence across RCTs was evaluated using the GRADE (Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development, and Evaluation) working group guidelines. The quality of evidence was then divided into four categories based on the corresponding evaluation criteria: high, moderate, low, and very low.28

sec.title: Statistical meta-analysis
sec.p: The difference between the mean of the baseline and endpoint variables in each arm was extracted, and the weighted mean difference (WMD) and standard deviation (SD) of the difference between the two arms were selected as the effect size.29 In case that study did not directly report the difference between baseline and endpoint values, we calculate the change of mean and related SD using the following formula: SD change = square root [(SD baseline2 + SD final2)—(2 × 0.5 × SD baseline × SD final)].30 The WMDs were pooled using the random-effects model with restricted maximum likelihood estimation. Cochrane's Q statistic and Hedges I2 estimation were used to evaluate the heterogeneity across the articles.31 Heterogeneity among studies was assessed using Cochrane's Q statistic and I² statistic,31 with I² values classified as low (<25%), moderate (25–70%), or high (>70%) heterogeneity. Subgroup analyses were performed by follow-up duration (<24 months and ≥24 months). Sensitivity analyses were conducted using the "Leave-one-out" and common-effect models to assess the robustness of the results. All statistical analyses were performed using R Statistical Software with the "meta" package (v4.1.2; R Core Team 2021).

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p:
The study selection process followed the PRISMA guidelines and is illustrated in Figure 1. A total of 1,047 records were initially identified through electronic database searches, including PubMed (n = 121), Web of Science (n = 180), and Scopus (n = 756). After removing 151 duplicate records, 896 unique studies remained and were screened based on their titles and abstracts. During this stage, 886 records were excluded due to being review articles, nonhuman studies, or having irrelevant titles and abstracts. Subsequently, 10 full-text articles were assessed for eligibility. Of these, four studies were excluded because of insufficient data or the absence of a control group. Ultimately, only six RCTs met the inclusion criteria and were included in the final systematic review and meta-analysis.32, 33, 34, 35, 36, 37Figure 1
PRISMA Flow chart of the study selection process for inclusion studies in the systematic review.Figure 1 dummy alt text

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p:
The general characteristics of the six RCTs included in the systematic review and meta-analysis are summarized in Table 1. The studies were published between 2006 and 2024 and were conducted across diverse geographical regions, including Israel, the United States, China, Iraq, and Iran. Most trials employed a randomized double-blind design, while one study was single-blind and another did not report blinding status. All included studies enrolled adult patients diagnosed with MASLD, with some trials specifically targeting overweight or obese individuals and one including patients with metabolic syndrome. Sample sizes ranged from 41 to 130 participants, with intervention and control group sizes varying between 21 and 54 and 18 and 76, respectively. Across studies, participants were predominantly middle-aged adults, with mean ages ranging from approximately 43 to 48 years. Baseline BMI values indicated overweight to obese populations, with mean BMI ranging from 27.1 to 37.3 kg/m². Trial durations varied from 12 to 36 weeks. Orlistat was administered as the primary intervention in all studies, most commonly at a dose of 360 mg/day, although one study used a lower dose of 120 mg/day. Control groups received a placebo or standard/conventional therapy.Table 1
General characteristics of included studies in the systematic review and meta-analysis.Table 1 dummy alt text
StudyCountryStudy designPatientsSample size(IG / CG)SexAge (year)BMITrial DurationIntervention(dose)Control
IGCGIGCG
Zelber-Sagi et al.32IsraelRCT, DBMASLD44(21 / 23)M: 19, F: 2548.4 ± 8.147 ± 12.234.8 ± 531.5 ± 5.824 weeksOrlistat360 mg/dayPlacebo
Harrison et al.33USARCT, DBMASLDOverweight41(23 / 18)M: 13, F: 2847.9 ± 7.545.5 ± 10.737.3 ± 636 weeksOrlistat360 mg/dayControl
J. Ye et al.34ChinaRCT, DBMASLDObese130(54 /76)M: 88, F: 4245.4 ± 1345.4 ± 13.330.7 ± 329.9 ± 2.424 weeksOrlistat360 mg/dayConventional therapy
Esmail et al.35IraqRCT, SBMASLDMetS50(25 / 25)NR43.3 ± 9(overall)43.3 ± 9(overall)33.8 ± 5.6(overall)33.8 ± 5.6(overall)12 weeksOrlistat120 mg/dayPlacebo
X. Feng et al.36ChinaRCTMASLDObese71(32 / 39)M: 50, F: 2146 ± 1144 ± 1229.8 ± 2.728.3 ± 2.224 weeksOrlistat360 mg/dayControl
Zahmatkesh et al.37IranRCT, DBMASLDObese53(I27 /26)M: 11, F: 1614.14 ± 1.813.38 ± 1.627.1 ± 2.1226.7 ± 2.1612 weeksOrlistat360 mg/dayPlacebo
RCT = randomized controlled trial; SB = single-blind; DB = double-blind; IG. = intervention group; CG. = control group; M = male; F = female; MASLD = metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease; MetS = metabolic syndrome; NR = not reported.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p:
The quality assessment using the Cochrane RoB-2 revealed that, while most randomized controlled trials demonstrated a low risk of bias in domains such as randomization and outcome reporting, several studies raised concerns in areas such as allocation concealment, blinding of participants and personnel, and measurement of outcomes. Although not uniformly present across studies, these methodological limitations underscore the need for cautious interpretation of the pooled findings. Sensitivity analyses further indicated that excluding studies with higher risks did not materially alter the overall effect estimates, thereby supporting a moderate level of confidence in the synthesized evidence despite the observed biases (Figure 2).Figure 2
Quality assessment of studies according to Cochrane risk-of-bias 2 (RoB-2).Figure 2 dummy alt text

sec.title: Meta-analysis: orlistat on liver enzymes
sec.p:
The pooled analysis showed that Orlistat significantly reduced markers of liver injury in patients with MASLD. For AST, the overall WMD was −4.01 U/L (95% CI: −6.05 to −1.97, P = 0.005) with no observed heterogeneity (I² = 0.0%, P = 0.62). ALT also decreased significantly with a WMD of −8.70 U/L (95% CI: −16.51 to −0.90, P = 0.036) and moderate heterogeneity (I² = 29.9%, P = 0.22). Subgroup analysis for interventions ≥24 months demonstrated a greater reduction in ALT (−14.20 U/L, 95% CI: −21.11 to −7.30, P < 0.01) and AST (−5.97 U/L, 95% CI: −9.25 to −2.69, P = 0.01), both with negligible heterogeneity. GGT was also significantly reduced (WMD: −7.74 U/L, 95% CI: −14.88 to −0.59, P = 0.04, I² = 0.0%), whereas ALP showed no significant change (Figure 3, Table 2).Figure 3
Forest plot demonstrating weighted mean difference (WMD) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for the effect of Orlistat administration on serum liver enzymes, including aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), and gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT), in patients with MASLD.Figure 3 dummy alt textTable 2
Meta-analysis findings for the efficacy of orlistat on cardiometabolic indices in patients with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD).Table 2 dummy alt text
Variable groupNo. of ESWMD95% CIP-value1Heterogeneity (I2)P-value2
Systolic blood pressure (SBP: mmHg)

WMD = weighted mean difference; CI = confidence interval; AST = aspartate aminotransferase; ALT = alanine aminotransferase; ALP = alkaline phosphatase; GGT = gamma-glutamyl transferase; TG = triglycerides; TC = total cholesterol; LDL-C = low-density lipoprotein cholesterol; HDL-C = high-density lipoprotein cholesterol; FBG = fasting blood glucose; FI = fasting insulin; HOMA-IR = homeostatic model assessment for insulin resistance; BMI = body mass index; BP = blood pressure.
*Bold values with asterisk indicate statistically significant (P < 0-05).

sec.title: Meta-analysis: orlistat on lipid profile
sec.p:
Orlistat demonstrated favorable effects on lipid parameters, with significant reductions in LDL-C and trends toward improvement in other lipids. The overall WMD for LDL-C was −5.61 mg/dL (95% CI: −7.36 to −3.86, P = 0.002) with no heterogeneity (I² = 0.0%). Subgroup analysis for ≥24 months interventions confirmed a similar significant reduction (WMD: −5.59 mg/dL, 95% CI: −8.88 to −2.31, P = 0.02). HDL-C showed a nonsignificant overall increase (WMD: 0.92 mg/dL, 95% CI: −0.44 to 2.29, P = 0.101, I² = 17.6%), but in long-term interventions (≥24 months), the increase was significant (WMD: 1.65 mg/dL, 95% CI: 0.95 to 2.36, P = 0.02, I² = 0.0%). TC and TG showed nonsignificant overall changes, though heterogeneity was moderate for TC (I² = 31.6%) and TG (I² = 51.8%). Overall, Orlistat improves LDL-C and HDL-C, with more pronounced effects over longer treatment periods (Figure 4, Table 2).Figure 4
Forest plot demonstrating weighted mean difference (WMD) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for the effect of Orlistat administration on serum lipid profile, including triglycerides (TG), total cholesterol (TC), low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C), and high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C), in patients with MASLD.Figure 4 dummy alt text

sec.title: Meta-analysis: orlistat on glycemic markers
sec.p:
Orlistat significantly improved glycemic control in patients with MASLD. FBS decreased overall by −4.05 mg/dL (95% CI: −6.32 to −1.78, P = 0.005) with no heterogeneity (I² = 0.0%). FI showed a significant reduction of −2.50 μU/mL (95% CI: −3.46 to −1.55, P = 0.003, I² = 0.0%), and HOMA-IR decreased by −0.70 (95% CI: −0.98 to −0.41, P = 0.004, I² = 0.0%). Subgroup analyses indicated that interventions lasting ≥24 months maintained significant improvements in HOMA-IR (−0.55, 95% CI: −1.02 to −0.09, P = 0.03), while reductions in FBS and FI were not statistically significant in this subgroup. Short-term interventions (<24 months) demonstrated nonsignificant trends. These findings suggest Orlistat improves insulin sensitivity and glycemic indices, particularly over extended treatment periods (Figure 5, Table 2).Figure 5
Forest plot demonstrating weighted mean difference (WMD) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for the effect of Orlistat administration on glycemic markers, including fasting blood glucose (FBG), fasting insulin, and homeostatic model assessment for insulin resistance (HOMA-IR) in patients with MASLD.Figure 5 dummy alt text

sec.title: Meta-analysis: orlistat on BP
sec.p:
Orlistat had no significant effect on BP. The overall weighted mean difference for systolic BP was 0.98 mm Hg (95% CI: −3.49 to 5.46, P = 0.53, I² = 0.0%) and for diastolic BP was −1.32 mm Hg (95% CI: −7.69 to 5.04, P = 0.46, I² = 0.0%). Subgroup analyses by intervention duration (≥24 months and <24 months) also showed no statistically significant changes, with negligible heterogeneity in all comparisons. These results indicate that Orlistat does not significantly alter BP in patients with MASLD (Figure 6, Table 2).Figure 6
Forest plot demonstrating weighted mean difference (WMD) 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for the effect of Orlistat administration on systolic and diastolic blood pressure (BP) in patients with MASLD.Figure 6 dummy alt text

sec.title: Meta-analysis: orlistat on BMI
sec.p:
Orlistat treatment resulted in a significant reduction in BMI. The overall WMD was −0.99 kg/m² (95% CI: −1.18 to −0.81, P < 0.001) with no heterogeneity (I² = 0.0%). Subgroup analysis revealed that interventions lasting ≥24 months showed similar significant reductions in BMI (−0.96 kg/m², 95% CI: −1.22 to −0.70, P < 0.01), whereas shorter interventions (<24 months) demonstrated a nonsignificant trend (−1.25 kg/m², 95% CI: −3.64 to 1.13, P = 0.09) (Figure 7, Table 2).Figure 7
Forest plot demonstrating weighted mean difference (WMD) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for the effect of Orlistat administration on body mass index (BMI) in patients with MASLD.Figure 7 dummy alt text

sec.title: Sensitivity analyses
sec.p:
Figure 8 presents the results of sensitivity analyses using the leave-one-out method to evaluate the robustness of the meta-analysis findings for cardiometabolic indices in patients with MASLD. For AST, ALP, HOMA-IR, BMI, systolic BP, and diastolic BP, omitting any single study did not materially change the pooled estimates, indicating robust and stable results. However, for ALT, the pooled effect size was sensitive to the removal of Zelber-Sagi et al.,32 Harrison et al.,33 or J. Ye et al.,34 suggesting that these studies had a notable influence on the overall result. In the case of GGT, removing any included study significantly affected the pooled estimate. For lipid outcomes (TG, TC, HDL-C, LDL-C), excluding Feng et al.36 substantially altered the pooled mean differences. Similarly, the pooled effects for FBG and FI were significantly influenced by the removal of Zahmatkesh et al.37 These findings demonstrate that while some outcomes (e.g., AST, ALP, BMI, BP) are robust, others (e.g., ALT, GGT, lipids, FBG, FI) are sensitive to individual studies, highlighting areas where further high-quality trials may strengthen the evidence.Figure 8
Sensitivity analyses based on the Leave-one-out method for the effect of Orlistat administration on cardiometabolic indices, including AST, ALT, ALP, GGT, TG, TC, LDL-C, HDL-C, FBG, HOMA-IR, BMI, BP in patients with MASLD.Figure 8 dummy alt text

sec.title: Grading of evidence
sec.p:
The GRADE assessment of Orlistat in MASLD patients shows high-quality evidence for liver enzymes (AST, ALT, ALP, GGT) and BMI, indicating confidence in these outcomes. However, lipid profile outcomes (TG, TC, HDL-C) and BP (systolic, diastolic) are rated moderate to low due to serious inconsistency and imprecision, reflecting uncertainty. Glycemic markers (FBG, FI, HOMA) show mixed quality, with FBG rated high but FI and HOMA rated low due to imprecision. Overall, evidence varies, with some outcomes more reliable than others (Table 3).Table 3
GRADE profile for the efficacy of orlistat on cardiometabolic indices in patients with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD).Table 3 dummy alt text
OutcomesRisk of biasInconsistencyIndirectnessImprecisionPublication BiasQuality of evidence
ASTModerate riskNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitation⊕⊕⊕⊕High
ALTModerate riskNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitation⊕⊕⊕⊕High
ALPModerate riskNo serious limitationNo serious limitationSerious limitation3No serious limitation⊕⊕⊕⊕High
GGTModerate riskNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitation⊕⊕⊕⊕High
TGModerate riskSerious limitation3No serious limitationSerious limitation3No serious limitation⊕⊕◯◯Low
TCModerate riskNo serious limitationNo serious limitationSerious limitation3No serious limitation⊕⊕⊕◯Moderate
HDLModerate riskNo serious limitationNo serious limitationSerious limitation3No serious limitation⊕⊕⊕◯Moderate
LDLModerate riskNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitation⊕⊕⊕⊕High
FBSModerate riskNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitation⊕⊕⊕⊕High
FIModerate riskNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitation⊕⊕⊕⊕High
HOMA-IRModerate riskNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitation⊕⊕⊕⊕High
Systolic BPModerate riskNo serious limitationNo serious limitationSerious limitation3Serious limitation⊕⊕◯◯Low
Diastolic BPModerate riskNo serious limitationNo serious limitationSerious limitation3Serious limitation⊕⊕◯◯Low
BMIModerate riskNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitationNo serious limitation⊕⊕⊕⊕High
WMD = weighted mean difference; CI = confidence interval; AST = aspartate aminotransferase; ALT = alanine aminotransferase; ALP = alkaline phosphatase; GGT = gamma-glutamyl transferase; TG = triglycerides; TC = total cholesterol; LDL-C = low-density lipoprotein cholesterol; HDL-C = high-density lipoprotein cholesterol; FBG = fasting blood glucose; FI = fasting insulin; HOMA-IR = homeostatic model assessment for insulin resistance; BMI = body mass index; BP = blood pressure.
1. There is a highly significant heterogeneity (I2 > 75).
2. There is a moderately significant heterogeneity (I2 > 45).
3. There is no evidence of significant effects of spirulina consumption.
4. There is a significant publication bias.3
p < 0.05

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This GRADE-assessed systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to evaluate the efficacy of orlistat on cardiometabolic indices in patients with MASLD. The pooled evidence from six RCTs demonstrates that orlistat confers significant benefits on several key metabolic and hepatic parameters, including liver enzymes (AST, ALT, and GGT), LDL-C, glycemic markers (FBG, FI, and HOMA-IR), and BMI, while exerting no significant effect on BP. Collectively, these findings support a potential adjunctive role for orlistat in the metabolic management of MASLD, particularly in overweight and obese individuals.

sec.title: Effects on liver enzymes and hepatic function
sec.p: Liver enzymes such as AST, ALT, and GGT are widely used surrogate markers of hepatocellular injury and metabolic liver stress in MASLD.38 In the present meta-analysis, orlistat significantly reduced AST, ALT, and GGT levels, with more pronounced effects observed in longer-duration interventions. These findings are clinically relevant, as persistently elevated aminotransferases and GGT are associated with increased risk of disease progression, cardiometabolic complications, and all-cause mortality in MASLD populations.39

The observed reductions in liver enzymes may be partly explained by orlistat’s capacity to reduce intestinal fat absorption, thereby decreasing hepatic free fatty acid influx and lipid accumulation.33,39 Excess intrahepatic fat promotes lipotoxicity, oxidative stress, and inflammatory signaling, which collectively contribute to hepatocyte injury and enzyme elevation.40 By attenuating dietary fat absorption and promoting modest but sustained weight loss, orlistat may indirectly alleviate hepatic steatosis and metabolic stress, resulting in improved liver enzyme profiles. The lack of a significant effect on ALP suggests that orlistat’s hepatic benefits are more closely related to hepatocellular injury rather than cholestatic processes.

sec.title: Effects on lipid profile
sec.p: Notable alterations in biochemical markers, particularly lipid profile indices, have been observed among MASLD patients. It has been noted that, in clinical practice, performing sonographic evaluations when biochemical and lipid abnormalities are detected is crucial, as early diagnosis can help prevent and delay further complications.41 Additionally, MASLD is increasingly recognized as a contributor to atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, primarily through its impact on lipid metabolism. The absence of approved pharmaceutical treatments has led to significant investment in MASLD drug development.42 On the other hand, research in both healthy and obese individuals has shown that Orlistat can specifically inhibit dietary fat absorption by blocking triglyceride hydrolysis.43 Herein, the current study showed that Orlistat has a varied impact on lipid profiles in MASLD patients. The most notable result was the significant reduction in LDL-C. The effect on HDL-C was not significant overall, although longer treatment durations showed some benefit. Orlistat did not significantly affect TG or TC levels, although there was a trend toward reduced TC. The variation in results highlights the complexity of Orlistat’s impact on lipid metabolism. The effects of Orlistat on the lipid profile are favorable in other studies as well. For instance, Sahebkar et al reported in a meta-analysis that Orlistat treatment can reduce TC, LDL-C, HDL-C, and TG levels. The reductions in total and LDL-C were more pronounced in patients who experienced greater weight loss and underwent shorter durations of Orlistat treatment.44 Regarding the effects of Orlistat on lipid profile parameters, in the study by Gabriel et al, Orlistat treatment effectively reduced prolonged postprandial increases in TG and very-low-density lipoprotein (VLDL) and lowered postprandial LDL-C levels, suggesting its potential to prevent cardiovascular diseases associated with postprandial lipemia.45

sec.title: Effects on glycemic markers and insulin resistance
sec.p: MASLD and type 2 diabetes are closely linked, frequently coexisting and exacerbating each other’s progression. Type 2 diabetes significantly increases the risk of MASLD advancing to more severe conditions, such as advanced fibrosis/cirrhosis. Evidence shows that individuals with MASLD are approximately twice as likely to develop type 2 diabetes, even when common metabolic risk factors such as obesity are absent, highlighting a strong independent association between the two conditions.46 Given the importance of glycemic control in MASLD, we aimed to explore the impact of Orlistat (due to its promising metabolic effects) on glycemic markers in individuals with MASLD. The present meta-analysis indicated that Orlistat can effectively reduce FBG, HOMA-IR, and FI levels, suggesting its potential to improve glycemic control in MASLD patients. The findings underscore Orlistat’s role in managing glycemic parameters, though the impact of dosage and duration on its effectiveness remains to be explored to optimize treatment strategies. A multicenter study has also highlighted the promising effects of Orlistat on glycemic parameters, although it was done in overweight patients with type 2 diabetes. The study demonstrated that over a year, Orlistat led to significantly greater weight loss than placebo and improved key markers of glycemic control, including reductions in HbA1c, fasting glucose, and postprandial glucose levels. Additionally, Orlistat resulted in a greater decrease in LDL-C.47 Another meta-analysis also reported that orlistat, when combined with diet, can improve glycemic control in overweight patients with type 2 diabetes. It suggested that Orlistat not only helps reduce body weight but also improves markers of glycemic control, such as blood sugar levels and HbA1c.48

sec.title: Effects on blood pressure
sec.p: Hypertension is another frequently linked condition with MASLD.49 Clinical studies have suggested a two-way relationship between hypertension and MASLD. The shared biological pathways and common genes detected through the current systems biology analysis may partially explain the molecular connections between these two diseases.50 The current meta-analysis found no statistically significant effect of Orlistat on BP in MASLD patients. Overall, these results indicate that Orlistat's effects on BP need further investigation. On the other hand, Orlistat has shown a different impact on BP across disease populations. For instance, a meta-analysis that investigated Orlistat's effects on obese adults with uncontrolled hypertension found that Orlistat promoted significant weight loss, which was associated with notable reductions in both BP and heart rate. The study suggested that Orlistat could be useful in managing hypertension in overweight and obese individuals, extending its benefits beyond just weight loss.51 However, in another meta-analysis, diabetic patients treated with Orlistat saw smaller and nonsignificant reductions in BP compared to nondiabetic patients. Specifically, SBP and DBP reductions were less pronounced in the diabetic group, highlighting a potentially reduced efficacy of Orlistat in lowering BP among individuals with diabetes.52

sec.title: Effects on body weight
sec.p: Orlistat significantly reduced BMI in patients with MASLD, with consistent effects across studies and greater benefits observed with longer treatment duration. Although the absolute reduction in BMI was modest, even small degrees of sustained weight loss are associated with meaningful improvements in hepatic steatosis, insulin resistance, and cardiometabolic outcomes.53,54 Importantly, weight reduction remains the cornerstone of MASLD management, and pharmacologic agents that facilitate adherence to lifestyle interventions may offer added clinical value.

sec.title: Strengths and limitations
sec.p: This study has several notable strengths. To our knowledge, this is one of the most comprehensive and methodologically rigorous meta-analyses evaluating the effects of orlistat on cardiometabolic indices, specifically in patients with MASLD, and incorporates only randomized controlled trials. The review was conducted in accordance with PRISMA guidelines, prospectively registered in PROSPERO, and applied a predefined PICOS framework, enhancing transparency and reproducibility. The use of random-effects models, subgroup analyses stratified by dose and treatment duration, leave-one-out sensitivity analyses, and the GRADE methodology further strengthened the robustness and interpretability of the findings. Moreover, the inclusion of multiple clinically relevant outcomes—liver enzymes, lipid profile, glycemic indices, BMI, and BP provides a comprehensive evaluation of orlistat’s metabolic effects beyond weight loss alone.

Nevertheless, several limitations should be acknowledged. Only six RCTs were eligible for inclusion, which limited statistical power for some outcomes and precluded formal assessment of publication bias. Variability in study design, sample size, intervention duration, and methodological quality contributed to heterogeneity in certain outcomes, and sensitivity analyses indicated that some results were influenced by individual studies. Additionally, most included trials had relatively short follow-up periods, restricting conclusions regarding long-term efficacy and safety. The reliance on surrogate metabolic markers rather than histological or imaging-based endpoints reflects current constraints in MASLD research and may limit direct inference on disease progression.23,24 Finally, sex-specific effects and menopausal status were not examined, despite MASLD being a sexually dimorphic condition55,56 underscoring the need for future well-powered trials to address these gaps.

sec.title: Clinical implications
sec.p: The findings of this meta-analysis suggest that Orlistat may be a valuable therapeutic option for patients with MASLD, as it improves key metabolic and liver-related parameters. Clinically, Orlistat demonstrated significant reductions in liver enzymes, LDL-C, FBG, FI, HOMA-IR, and BMI, indicating its potential to improve liver function, glycemic control, and overall metabolic health. These effects highlight Orlistat’s role not only as an antiobesity agent but also as a pharmacologic strategy for managing the systemic metabolic complications associated with MASLD. However, the evidence also reveals variability in lipid profiles and BP outcomes, underscoring the need for individualized patient assessment and careful monitoring during therapy.

sec.title: Future research directions
sec.p: Future research should focus on larger, multicenter RCTs with longer follow-up periods to assess the long-term efficacy and safety of Orlistat in MASLD patients. Studies examining optimal dosing strategies, the impact of treatment duration, and patient subgroups, including differences in sex, age, baseline metabolic status, and presence of comorbidities such as type 2 diabetes, are warranted. Furthermore, integrating imaging-based and histological endpoints alongside metabolic biomarkers could provide a more comprehensive understanding of Orlistat’s effects on hepatic fat content, fibrosis progression, and cardiovascular risk. Investigating the mechanistic pathways underlying Orlistat’s metabolic benefits, beyond weight loss, may also inform precision-medicine approaches and guide combination therapies for MASLD management.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This GRADE-assessed systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that orlistat is associated with significant improvements in key cardiometabolic and hepatic indices in patients with MASLD. Orlistat significantly reduced liver enzymes (AST, ALT, and GGT), improved glycemic markers (FBG, fasting insulin, and HOMA-IR), lowered LDL-C, and resulted in a modest but consistent reduction in BMI, while showing no significant effect on BP. These findings highlight orlistat’s potential role as an adjunctive pharmacologic option for metabolic risk modification in MASLD, extending beyond its role in weight loss alone. However, given the limited number of available RCTs and variability in study duration and quality, larger well-designed trials with longer follow-up and clinically relevant hepatic endpoints are required to confirm their long-term efficacy and safety in this population.

sec.title: Data Availability
sec.p: The data supporting this study's findings are derived from publicly available studies and are reported in the articles and supplementary materials. For further inquiries, please contact the corresponding authors.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: M.K. developed the research idea and led the literature search. S.P. and M.K. screened the studies, conducted the research, and extracted the relevant data. S.M.A.P. and M.K. performed the statistical analysis and interpreted the data. P.P.R., S.M.A.P., S.P., and M.K. contributed to developing and drafting the initial manuscript. M.K. reviewed and revised the manuscript and supervised the project. All authors critically read the final manuscript and approved it for submission.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: We would like to express our sincere gratitude to individuals and the researchers whose work is included in this study for their invaluable contributions to this article.

fn.p: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.curtheres.2026.100822.
